---
title: "Try It Yourself!"
subtitle: "pandas groupby ↔ dplyr pipes, with a SQL cameo"
webr:
  show-startup-message: false
---
```{webr-r}
#| context: setup

library(dplyr)
```
<style>
.reveal-btn {
  background-color: #4CAF50;
  color: white;
  padding: 10px 20px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 8px;
  cursor: pointer;
  font-size: 16px;
  transition: background-color 0.3s, transform 0.2s;
}
.reveal-btn:hover {
  background-color: #45a049;
  transform: scale(1.05);
}
.reveal-btn:active {
  transform: scale(0.98);
}
.prompt-box, .ai-response-box {
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 12px 16px;
  margin: 12px 0;
}
.prompt-box {
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-left: 4px solid #6c757d;
}
.ai-response-box {
  background-color: #eef6ff;
  border-left: 4px solid #4A90E2;
}
.prompt-label, .ai-label {
  font-weight: bold;
  display: block;
  margin-bottom: 6px;
}
.prompt-label { color: #6c757d; }
.ai-label { color: #4A90E2; }
.match-table {
  width: 100%;
  border-collapse: collapse;
  margin: 15px 0;
}
.match-table th, .match-table td {
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
  padding: 10px;
  text-align: left;
  vertical-align: middle;
}
.match-table th {
  background-color: #4A90E2;
  color: white;
}
.match-table code { white-space: nowrap; }
.match-select {
  width: 100%;
  padding: 5px;
  border-radius: 5px;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
}
.feedback-icon {
  font-size: 18px;
  margin-left: 8px;
}
.callout-tip {
  border-left: 4px solid #4CAF50;
  background: #f0fff0;
  border-radius: 5px;
  padding: 10px 16px;
  margin: 12px 0;
}
.three-col {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 1rem;
  margin: 1rem 0;
}
.lang-card {
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 12px 14px;
  font-size: 13px;
}
.lang-card.r    { background: #fff3f3; border-left: 4px solid #e05c5c; }
.lang-card.py   { background: #eef6ff; border-left: 4px solid #4A90E2; }
.lang-card.sql  { background: #fffbe6; border-left: 4px solid #e6a817; }
.lang-card h4   { margin: 0 0 8px; font-size: 13px; }
.lang-card pre  { margin: 0; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6);
                  padding: 6px 8px; border-radius: 4px; overflow-x: auto; }
</style>

## Share your thoughts

As you are trying, please, share your thoughts, challenges, AI outputs, etc. on a new slide in [this slidedeck](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1mI8xHcWrm5yyu6YatNTdiBneGG3GNxB7CitujsBBhXk/edit?usp=sharing)


## The shared dataset

We'll use a small sales dataset: transactions across three product categories
over several months. Define it once in each language — the analysis is identical.

::: panel-tabset
## R

```{webr-r}
sales <- data.frame(
  month    = c("Jan","Jan","Jan","Feb","Feb","Feb","Mar","Mar","Mar"),
  category = c("Books","Electronics","Clothing",
                "Books","Electronics","Clothing",
                "Books","Electronics","Clothing"),
  revenue  = c(120, 340, 210, 95, 410, 180, 150, 290, 230),
  units    = c(12, 4, 15, 9, 5, 13, 14, 3, 17)
)
head(sales)
```

## Python

```{pyodide-python}
import pandas as pd

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "month":    ["Jan","Jan","Jan","Feb","Feb","Feb","Mar","Mar","Mar"],
    "category": ["Books","Electronics","Clothing",
                 "Books","Electronics","Clothing",
                 "Books","Electronics","Clothing"],
    "revenue":  [120, 340, 210, 95, 410, 180, 150, 290, 230],
    "units":    [12, 4, 15, 9, 5, 13, 14, 3, 17]
})
sales.head()
```
:::

---

## Part 1: Group, summarise, and sort

The most common data-wrangling task: **total revenue by category, sorted descending**.

### In R — the dplyr pipe

```{webr-r}
sales |>
  group_by(category) |>
  summarise(
    total_revenue = sum(revenue),
    total_units   = sum(units)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_revenue))
```

### In Python — pandas groupby

```{pyodide-python}
(sales
  .groupby("category")
  .agg(total_revenue=("revenue", "sum"),
       total_units=("units",   "sum"))
  .sort_values("total_revenue", ascending=False)
  .reset_index()
)
```

### Side-by-side comparison

::: {.callout-tip}
**Key idiom differences**

| Concept | dplyr (R) | pandas (Python) |
|---|---|---|
| Start a chain | `df |> ...` | `(df...)`  |
| Group rows | `group_by(category)` | `.groupby("category")` |
| Summarise | `summarise(name = fn(col))` | `.agg(name=("col", "fn"))` |
| Sort descending | `arrange(desc(col))` | `.sort_values("col", ascending=False)` |
| Column names quoted? | Bare names: `category` | Always strings: `"category"` |
:::

---

## Part 2: Filter, then group

Now: **average revenue per unit, for months that aren't February**.

### In R

```{webr-r}
library(dplyr)

sales |>
  filter(month != "Feb") |>
  group_by(month, category) |>
  summarise(avg_rev_per_unit = mean(revenue / units), .groups = "drop")
```

### In Python

```{pyodide-python}
(sales
  .query('month != "Feb"')
  .assign(rev_per_unit=lambda df: df["revenue"] / df["units"])
  .groupby(["month", "category"])
  .agg(avg_rev_per_unit=("rev_per_unit", "mean"))
  .reset_index()
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**Idiom flags**

- `filter()` → `.query()` — both accept an expression, but pandas requires a string.
- `mutate()` → `.assign()` — pandas uses a `lambda` to reference the DataFrame mid-chain.
- `.groups = "drop"` in dplyr → `.reset_index()` in pandas — both un-nest the groups.
:::

---

## Part 3: SQL ↔ tidyverse cameo

The same aggregation in all three languages. Notice how SQL is the conceptual
middle ground — its `GROUP BY` maps directly to both `group_by()` and `.groupby()`.

<div class="three-col">
<div class="lang-card sql">
<h4>🗄 SQL</h4>
<pre>
SELECT
  category,
  SUM(revenue)  AS total_revenue,
  SUM(units)    AS total_units
FROM sales
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
</pre>
</div>
<div class="lang-card r">
<h4>📦 dplyr (R)</h4>
<pre>
sales |>
  group_by(category) |>
  summarise(
    total_revenue = sum(revenue),
    total_units   = sum(units)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_revenue))
</pre>
</div>
<div class="lang-card py">
<h4>🐍 pandas (Python)</h4>
<pre>
(sales
  .groupby("category")
  .agg(
    total_revenue=("revenue","sum"),
    total_units  =("units",  "sum"))
  .sort_values("total_revenue",
               ascending=False)
  .reset_index()
  )
</pre>
</div>
</div>

| SQL clause | dplyr verb | pandas method |
|---|---|---|
| `WHERE` | `filter()` | `.query()` |
| `SELECT col, fn(col)` | `summarise()` | `.agg()` |
| `GROUP BY` | `group_by()` | `.groupby()` |
| `ORDER BY ... DESC` | `arrange(desc(...))` | `.sort_values(..., ascending=False)` |
| `AS name` | `name = expr` inside `summarise()` | `name=("col","fn")` inside `.agg()` |

---

## Match the syntax!

For each dplyr snippet, choose its pandas equivalent.

```{=html}
<table class="match-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>dplyr (R)</th><th>pandas (Python)</th><th></th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><code>filter(month == "Jan")</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="b" data-feedback="fb1">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.filter(month="Jan")</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.query('month == "Jan"')</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>.where("month", "Jan")</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb1" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>group_by(category)</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="a" data-feedback="fb2">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.groupby("category")</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.group("category")</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>.aggregate("category")</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb2" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>summarise(total = sum(revenue))</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="c" data-feedback="fb3">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.summarise(total=sum("revenue"))</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.sum("revenue")</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>.agg(total=("revenue", "sum"))</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb3" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>arrange(desc(revenue))</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="b" data-feedback="fb4">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.sort_values("revenue")</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.sort_values("revenue", ascending=False)</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>.order_by("revenue", desc=True)</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb4" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>mutate(rev_per_unit = revenue / units)</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="a" data-feedback="fb5">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.assign(rev_per_unit=lambda df: df["revenue"] / df["units"])</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.mutate(rev_per_unit="revenue/units")</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>.add_column("rev_per_unit", df["revenue"] / df["units"])</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb5" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>select(month, revenue)</code></td>
      <td>
        <select class="match-select" data-correct="c" data-feedback="fb6">
          <option value="">-- choose --</option>
          <option value="a"><code>.select(["month", "revenue"])</code></option>
          <option value="b"><code>.keep("month", "revenue")</code></option>
          <option value="c"><code>[["month", "revenue"]]</code></option>
        </select>
      </td>
      <td><span id="fb6" class="feedback-icon"></span></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<button class="reveal-btn" onclick="checkMatches()">Check Answers</button>
<p id="match-score" style="font-weight: bold; margin-top: 10px;"></p>

<script>
function checkMatches() {
  const selects = document.querySelectorAll('.match-select');
  let correct = 0;
  selects.forEach(sel => {
    const fb = document.getElementById(sel.dataset.feedback);
    if (sel.value === "") {
      fb.textContent = "";
    } else if (sel.value === sel.dataset.correct) {
      fb.textContent = "✅";
      correct++;
    } else {
      fb.textContent = "❌";
    }
  });
  document.getElementById('match-score').textContent =
    `You got ${correct} out of ${selects.length} correct!`;
}
</script>
```

---

## Your turn — fill in the blanks

Here's the complete R version of a new task: **total units sold per month, keeping
only months with more than 30 total units**.

```{webr-r}
library(dplyr)

sales |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(total_units = sum(units)) |>
  filter(total_units > 30) |>
  arrange(month)
```

Now complete the pandas equivalent below and run it:

```{pyodide-python}
sales
  .groupby(____)
  .agg(total_units=(____, ____))
  .reset_index()
  .query("____ > 30")
  .sort_values(____)

```

<button class="reveal-btn" onclick="document.getElementById('solution').style.display='block'">
Reveal Solution
</button>

::: {#solution style="display:none; margin-top:10px; padding:10px; border-left:4px solid #4CAF50; background:#f0fff0; border-radius:5px;"}
```python
sales
  .groupby("month")
  .agg(total_units=("units", "sum"))
  .reset_index()
  .query("total_units > 30")
  .sort_values("month")

```
:::

---

## Concept check

In dplyr you can write `filter(total_units > 30)` **immediately** after
`summarise()` because the pipe passes the result forward as a new data frame.
In pandas, why do you need `.reset_index()` **before** `.query("total_units > 30")`
— and what happens if you skip it?
